24.10.22.
"Goyda!!!"
Painting. Semyon Skrepetsky, oil on canvas. 80/60 cm.

In 2022, many Russian oppositionists turned into Russian patriots, explaining it by saying that earlier they hadn't liked Putin because they thought he wasn't Stalin enough, and only after the attack on Ukraine did the oppositionists understand that Putin is a real leader and conceive a flaming love for him.

Skrepetsky Picture Gallery.
303:
27.10.2022
"The Ash Trader."
Semyon Skrepetsky. Painting, oil on canvas. 60/40 cm.

Alexander Nevzorov. A hereditary Chekist, propagandist, friend of Putin.
Before the war he worked for Echo of Gazprom and played the role of an oppositionist. In 2012 he was Putin's representative in the presidential elections.
After the start of the Russian invasion of Ukraine, Nevzorov was embedded into the Ukrainian information space under the guise of a Ukrainian propagandist and worked off Kremlin talking points. He received Ukrainian citizenship by order of Zelensky. The first year of the war he scared Ukrainians with a nuclear strike that Putin was about to deliver on Kyiv. The second, he stoked ethnic hatred between Ukrainians and Russians. The third year he pumped Ukrainians full of hatred toward US President Donald Trump.
Cunning and slippery. Despite the fact that Nevzorov did everything to stoke ethnic hatred between Ukrainians and Russians, throughout the entire war he himself advertised Russian firms and goods on his Telegram channel, which had more than a million subscribers.
304:
"The Philosophers' Ship, 100 Years Later."
Semyon Skrepetsky. Painting, oil on canvas.
70/60 cm.
February 2025.

"The Philosophers' Ship" is the collective name for two voyages of German passenger vessels that, in September–November 1922, delivered representatives of the Russian intelligentsia — forcibly expelled from Soviet Russia — from Petrograd to Stettin (Germany).

Exactly 100 years later, the next "Philosophers' Ship" set off from Russia, but now with completely different contents.

Semyon Skrepetsky, painting, oil on canvas. 60/50 cm.

Outwardly weak plants possess an incredible will and life force that helps the tender flowers break through stone to burst out of the darkness onto the surface of the earth, where the flowers can enjoy the sun's rays and give offspring.

4 March 2025.

308:
"In the GULAG Everything Is Stable."

Russia and Ukraine have always been bound by two common spiritual staples — a mad love for leaders and an absolute, unblemished hatred of the USA.

The painting is based on the events of 28 February 2025. Vladimir Zelensky made a scene in the Oval Office, argued with the US president, was rude and swore, after which he was thrown out of the White House. US aid to Ukraine was immediately halted. Supplies of weapons and ammunition were frozen. Vladimir Zelensky's ego turned out to be above the national interests of a country defending itself. Many Ukrainians approved of Zelensky's behavior and supported him. Ukrainians began demonstratively destroying American products and tearing up clothes made in the USA.

316:
"A Treat From the Suitcase."
Semyon Skrepetsky, painting, oil on canvas. 70/50 cm.
19 March 2025

When Ksyusha was little, her godfather Uncle Volodya was a frequent guest in their home. Ksyusha's dad and Uncle Volodya were friends and worked together.
One day Uncle Volodya came when dad wasn't home and asked Ksyusha what she dreamed of. Ksyusha answered that she wanted to be a princess when she grew up.
Uncle Volodya smiled kindly and said he knew one sure way to become a princess and even a queen, but for that the girl had to eat treats from a suitcase. Ksyusha believed him, because she knew adults never lie, and asked Uncle Volodya to give her a treat. The godfather gladly threw open the suitcase and treated Ksyusha, while strictly forbidding her to tell anyone about the suitcase, because it would be their little secret.

The years went by. Every time Uncle Volodya came to visit, he quietly slipped into Ksyusha's room and treated his goddaughter to suitcase treats. Ksyusha grew up, but never became a princess; yet she can no longer do without the suitcase treats.

Uncle Volodya suffered from paranoid schizophrenia and believed that the special services of the whole world were hunting for his biomaterials, so from a young age he made it a rule never to leave his secretions and feces anywhere. Vladimir carefully collected everything that came out of his body in jars and carried it in his suitcase. So that the biomaterials would be destroyed, Volodya mixed them into the food of himself and his loved ones.

318:
"The Specter of Communism."
Semyon Skrepetsky, painting, oil on canvas. 50/60 cm.

At the end of the 20th century it seemed to the whole world that communism had been cast down into hell, but at the beginning of the 21st century the monster broke out of the underworld and launched a new attack on human civilization. Anti-human communist ideas easily penetrated the unformed minds of the youth, telling them fairy tales about a cozy GULAG and the tastiest ice cream.
Taking advantage of impunity, leftists the world over, under the slogans "EQUALITY AND BROTHERHOOD," infiltrated politics and began to parasitize, destroying the states they had latched onto.
21 March 2025
319:
"The Foundations of Communism."
Semyon Skrepetsky, painting, oil on canvas.
100/70 cm.

Vladimir Putin thought for a long time about what ideology to give Russians, so that it would be convenient and practical, would match the Russian spirit and the traditions of glorious ancestors. And of course he concluded that there's no need to reinvent the wheel, for everything new is the well-forgotten old.
This canvas captures the moment when Vladimir Vladimirovich Putin acquaints himself with the foundations of communist ideology.

325:
25.12.2022
"Overseer of the Pentagon."
Painting.
Semyon Skrepetsky. Oil on canvas. 60/40 cm.

Andrei Andreevich Piontkovsky. Russian mathematician and crime-boss-ologist. Honorary member of Ramzan Kadyrov's academy of academic sciences. He invented Piontkovsky's mathematical theory — a demoted crime-boss is no longer a boss, and a degraded rooster is no longer a rooster.
Piontkovsky is famous for a huge number of predictions, not one of which came true. Even a broken clock shows the right time twice a day, but that's not about Andrei Piontkovsky, for Andryukhich is no clock to you, but a hereditary mathematician.
Andrei Andreevich's father — Andrei Andreevich Piontkovsky — was also an honorary mathematician at the court of Comrade Stalin and in his time proved, by scientific, mathematical means, that the free labor of prisoners in the GULAG is far more profitable than the labor of hired citizens.

330:
"Call Me President!"
Semyon Skrepetsky. Painting, oil on canvas. 60/40 cm.
09.04.2025

The Guardian:
Navalnaya asks foreign politicians to address her as president of Russia.

According to Guardian columnist Piers Robinson, who was present at a meeting between Yulia Navalnaya and former UK Prime Minister David Cameron, this is her standard demand when communicating with foreign politicians. "It surprised me. She asked to be addressed as president of Russia. Not as leader of the democratic forces, for example, no. Later I learned this is her standard request when dealing with Western and European politicians. In private conversation she always asks to be called president of Russia. Most often they agree, although it doesn't conform to official etiquette. It's very strange, but it gives her pleasure," Robinson writes.

332:
01.02.2023
"Lover of Mole-Moonshine." Semyon Skrepetsky. Oil on canvas. Painting. 48/36 cm.

"Mole-moonshine" — an ancient Russian alcoholic drink, very strong. It's a spirit tincture made on a mole or a shrew. The ancient Russians believed in the healing power of the mole, so they caught the underground beast and infused spirit on it. After the 1917 revolution the traditional drink was forgotten, but in 2022, due to restrictions on imported alcohol supplies, Russians revived the traditions of their ancestors and again came to love mole-moonshine.
